message.content: Adversaries may exploit SSH (Secure Shell) to gain remote access to systems using valid accounts, enabling them to perform actions as the logged-on user. SSH is a widely-used protocol for secure remote shell access, commonly found on Linux and macOS systems. Authentication can be managed via passwords or public-private key pairs. Various threat actors, including APT39, BlackTech, and FIN7, have leveraged SSH for lateral movement, data transfer, and remote command execution. Tools like PuTTY, Cobalt Strike, and Empire facilitate these activities. The impact of such exploitation includes unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and further compromise of network segments. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strong, unique passwords, using multi-factor authentication, regularly updating SSH configurations, monitoring for unusual login activities, and restricting SSH access to necessary users and systems only.
